Question / Feature: Age
Gender
Response Type / Scale: Multiple choice: Male / Female / Non-binary / Other / Prefer not to say
Notes: Optional for fairness analysis

Question / Feature: Current status
Response Type / Scale: Multiple choice: Student / Working full-time / Working part-time / Unemployed / Other
Notes: remove? since we are looking at university students

Question / Feature: Field of study / work (optional)
Notes: Useful for descriptive analysis only; not essential for modelling

Question / Feature: On average, how much time did you spend using your smartphone per day in the last 2 weeks?
Notes: Core usage intensity feature

Question / Feature: How often do you check your phone in a typical day? (estimate)
Notes: Better as categories than free numeric

Question / Feature: How soon do you usually check your phone after waking up?
Response Type / Scale: Multiple choice: Immediately / Within 5 min / 5–30 min / >30 min
Notes: Captures habitual dependence

Question / Feature: How often do you use your phone after 11 PM?
Response Type / Scale: Likert 1–5: Never / Rarely / Sometimes / Often / Almost every night
Notes: Important sleep-related risk feature

Question / Feature: When do you usually stop using your phone at night?
Response Type / Scale: Multiple choice: Before 10 PM / 10–11 PM / 11 PM–12 AM / 12–1 AM / After 1 AM
Notes: Can be correlated with late-night use; keep if you want more timing detail

Question / Feature: Which categories of apps do you use the most? (Select up to 3)
Response Type / Scale: Multiple choice: Social media / Messaging / Video or streaming / Gaming / Productivity or study / Web browsing / Shopping / Other
Notes: Easier and safer than asking exact app names

Question / Feature: Approximately how much time per day do you spend on social media apps?
Notes: Strong digital overuse indicator

Question / Feature: Approximately how much time per day do you spend watching short-form videos (TikTok/Reels/YouTube Shorts)?
Notes: Potentially redundant with social media time in shorter version

Question / Feature: I use my phone during meals.
Response Type / Scale: Likert 1–5: Never / Rarely / Sometimes / Often / Always
Notes: Measures inappropriate-context use

Question / Feature: I use my phone during lectures, meetings, or work.
I check my phone while studying or working on tasks.
Notes: Similar to work/lecture item but still useful

Question / Feature: Notifications interrupt my work or concentration.
Notes: Captures attentional disruption

Question / Feature: I check my phone while talking to friends or family in person.
Notes: Useful behaviourally, but weaker in shorter version

Question / Feature: I use my phone when I feel bored.
Response Type / Scale: Likert 1–5: Strongly Disagree / Disagree / Neutral / Agree / Strongly Agree
Notes: Strong trigger feature

Question / Feature: I use my phone when I feel stressed.
Notes: Strong emotional coping signal

Question / Feature: I use my phone to avoid doing difficult tasks.
Notes: Captures procrastination / avoidance

Question / Feature: I use my phone when I feel lonely.
Notes: Suggested for removal in shorter version: weaker predictor than boredom/stress and may overlap conceptually

Question / Feature: I use my phone to distract myself from negative emotions.
Notes: Suggested for removal in shorter version: overlaps with stress coping, may create redundancy / multicollinearity

Question / Feature: I use my phone in bed before going to sleep.
Response Type / Scale: Likert 1–5: Never / Rarely / Sometimes / Often / Almost every night

Question / Feature: Phone use delays the time I go to sleep.
Notes: Strong behavioural risk signal

Question / Feature: Notifications wake me up at night.
I wake up during the night to check my phone.
Notes: Important sleep disruption feature

Question / Feature: How would you rate your sleep quality over the past 2 weeks?
Response Type / Scale: Multiple choice: Very poor / Poor / Average / Good / Very good
Notes: Useful descriptively; may overlap with PHQ-9 sleep item

Question / Feature: I unlock my phone without a clear reason.
Response Type / Scale: Likert 1–5: Never / Rarely / Sometimes / Often / Very Often
Notes: Captures automatic checking

Question / Feature: I continue using my phone longer than I intended.
Notes: Loss-of-control feature

Question / Feature: I try to reduce my phone use but fail.
Notes: Important self-control signal

Question / Feature: I feel uncomfortable when I cannot access my phone.
Notes: Can overlap with SAS-SV dependence items

Question / Feature: I feel that my phone use is hard to control.
Notes: Broad control item; may overlap with SAS-SV

Question / Feature: I miss planned work due to smartphone use.
Notes: 1 = Strongly Disagree, 2 = Disagree, 3 = Weakly disagree, 4 = Weakly agree, 5 = Agree, 6 = Strongly Agree

Question / Feature: I have a hard time concentrating in class, while doing assignments, or while working due to smartphone use.
I feel pain in my wrists or at the back of my neck while using a smartphone.
I wouldn’t be able to stand not having a smartphone.
I feel impatient and fretful when I am not holding my smartphone.
I have my smartphone in my mind even when I am not using it.
I will never give up using my smartphone even when my daily life is affected by it.
I constantly check my smartphone so as not to miss conversations or notifications.
I use my smartphone longer than I had intended.
People around me tell me that I use my smartphone too much.
Notes: Main prediction target label comes from total SAS-SV score

Question / Feature: Little interest or pleasure in doing things
Notes: 0 = Not at all, 1 = Several days, 2 = More than half the days, 3 = Nearly every day

Question / Feature: Feeling down, depressed, or hopeless
Trouble falling or staying asleep, or sleeping too much
Feeling tired or having little energy
Poor appetite or overeating
Feeling bad about yourself — or that you are a failure or have let yourself or your family down
Trouble concentrating on things, such as reading or watching television
Moving or speaking so slowly that other people could have noticed, or the opposite — being fidgety or restless
Thoughts that you would be better off dead or of hurting yourself in some way
Notes: Safety escalation item
